Understanding Vector Databases

A vector database stores high-dimensional numerical representations of content, called embeddings, and retrieves them by similarity rather than by exact match. This makes it possible to search by meaning instead of by keyword, which is the foundation of retrieval-augmented generation.

Embeddings are produced by a model that maps text into a fixed-length vector, such that semantically related passages end up close together in that space. Distance between two vectors therefore approximates how related the underlying passages are. Cosine distance is the most common measure because it ignores magnitude and compares direction alone.

Approximate nearest neighbour indexes such as HNSW trade a small amount of recall for a very large speedup. HNSW builds a navigable small-world graph over the vectors, which can be constructed incrementally as rows arrive. IVFFlat instead partitions the space into clusters and must be built against data that already exists, which makes it a poor fit for a table that starts empty.

A common mistake is to omit a tenant filter from the similarity query. In a multi-tenant system this silently returns other people's data, because vector search has no notion of ownership on its own.
